title: Expected value with calculated probabilities
description: Find expected value based on calculated probabilities.
topic_path: Topic C: Lessons 13-19: Applications of expected value and probability

assessment_items.question_content:
You are in a basketball $3$-point contest. You have $1$ regular ball, and $1$ money ball left to shoot. If you make both shots, you will get a total of $10$ points. If you make just the money ball, you will get $3$ points. Under any other outcome, you will receive $0$ points.

You approximate that the probability of you making the regular shot is $60\%$ and the probability of you making the money ball is $70\%$. You assume that each shot is independent.

**What is the expected number of points you will score?**  
 Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.  
 [[☃ input-number 1]] points
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

We need to find the probability and value for each possible event.

The probability of you making both shots is:

$\qquad 0.6 \cdot 0.7  = 0.42$

The probability of you making just the money ball is:

$\qquad 0.4 \cdot 0.7 = 0.28$

The probability of any other event is:

$\qquad 1 - 0.42 - 0.28 = 0.3$

Event| Probability | Points 
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Make both shots| $0.42$ |  $10$ 
Make just the money ball| $0.28$ | $3$ 
Other| $0.3$|  $0$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(0.42 \cdot 10\right) + \left(0.28 \cdot 3\right)  + \left(0.3 \cdot 0\right) $
The expected number of points you will score is $5.04$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jessica is playing a game where there are $4$ blue markers and $6$ red markers in a box. She is going to pick $3$ markers without replacement.

If she picks all $3$ red markers, she will win a total of $\$500$. If the first marker she picks is red but not all $3$ markers are red, she will win a total of $\$100$. Under any other outcome, she will win $\$0$. 

**What is the expected value of Jessica's winnings?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible event.

The probability of Jessica picking $3$ consecutive red markers is:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{6}{10}\right)\left(\dfrac{5}{9}\right) \left(\dfrac{4}{8}\right) = \dfrac{1}{6}$

The probability of Jessica's first marker being red, but not picking $3$ consecutive red markers is:

$\qquad$  $\dfrac{6}{10} - \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{13}{30}$

The probability of any other event is:

$\qquad1 - \dfrac{1}{6} - \dfrac{13}{30} = \dfrac{2}{5}$

Event| Probability | Winnings
:-: | :-: | :-: 
All $3$ red markers | $\dfrac{1}{6}$ |  $\$500$ 
First is red but not all $3$|   $\dfrac{13}{30}$ | $\$100$ 
Other| $\dfrac{2}{5}$|  $\$0$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{1}{6} \cdot \$500\right) + \left(\dfrac{13}{30} \cdot \$100\right)  + \left(\dfrac{2}{5} \cdot \$0\right)$
Jessica's expected winnings are $\$126.67$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lucas is fishing in a pond where there are exactly $3$ walleye and $1$ catfish. He has an equal chance of catching each fish. If Lucas catches a catfish, the game warden will make him stop fishing because catfish are currently quite endangered in this pond.  

When Lucas catches a walleye, he keeps it so that he can feed his entire family. If he can catch all $3$ walleye in the pond, he can feed his family which is worth a total of $\$100$ to him. If he can catch $2$ walleye, he will only be able to feed himself, which is worth $\$20$ to him. Any other outcome is worth $\$0$ to Lucas.

**What is the expected value of Lucas going fishing? **   
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible event.

The probability of Lucas catching all $3$ walleye is:

$\qquad$ **$\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right) \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{1}{4}$**

The probability of Lucas catching exactly $2$ walleye is:

$\qquad$  **$\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right) - \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{1}{4}$**

The probability of any other event is:

$\qquad$ **$1 - \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$**

Event| Probability | Value
:-: | :-: | :-: 
$3$ walleye | $\dfrac{1}{4}$ |  $\$100$ 
Exactly $2$ walleye|   $\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $\$20$ 
Other| $\dfrac{1}{2}$|  $\$0$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \$100\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \$20\right)  + \left(\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \$0\right)$
The expected value of Lucas going fishing is $\$30$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ahmed is playing a lottery game where he must pick $2$ numbers from $0$ to $9$ and then one letter out of the $26$-letter English alphabet. (He may choose the same number both times.) 

If his ticket matches the $2$ numbers and $1$ letter drawn in order, he wins the grand prize and receives $\$10{,}405$. If just his letter matches but one or both of his numbers do not match, he wins the small prize of $\$100$. Under any other outcome, he loses and receives nothing. The game costs him $\$5$ to play.

 He has chosen the ticket $\text{04R}$. 

**What is the expected profit for Ahmed when he buys a ticket?**  
Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.   
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible event.

The probability of Ahmed winning the **grand prize** is:

$\qquad$ **$\left(\dfrac{1}{10}\right)\left(\dfrac{1}{10}\right) \left(\dfrac{1}{26}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2600}$**

The probability of Ahmed winning the **small prize** is:

$\qquad$  **$\dfrac{1}{26} - \dfrac{1}{2600} = \dfrac{99}{2600}$**

The probability of Ahmed **not winning any prize** is:

$\qquad$ **$1 - \dfrac{1}{2600} - \dfrac{99}{2600} = \dfrac{25}{26}$**

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{1}{2600} \cdot \$10{,}400\right) + \left(\dfrac{99}{2600} \cdot \$95\right)  + \left(\dfrac{25}{26} \cdot -\$5\right)$
The expected value of Ahmed's ticket is $\$2.81$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Wisconsin Badgers football team has $2$ regular season games and $1$ bowl game left in their season.

If they win all $3$ games, they will make a total of $100$ million dollars in revenue. If they win their bowl game, but not all $3$ games, they will make a total of $20$ million dollars in revenue. Under any other outcome, they will make just $10$ million dollars in revenue. 

Sports analysts are predicting that the Badgers  have a $60\%$ chance of winning each regular season game and a $40\%$ chance of winning their bowl game. Sports analysts assume that each game is independent. 

**What is the expected revenue in millions of dollars for the Wisconsin Badgers football team?**  
Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.   
[[☃ input-number 1]] million dollars
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible event.

The probability of The Wisconsin Badgers winning all $3$ games is:

$\qquad 0.6 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.4  = 0.144$

The probability of The Wisconsin Badgers winning their bowl game, but not all $3$ games is:

$\qquad 0.4 - 0.144 = 0.256$

The probability of any other event is:

$\qquad 1 - 0.144 - 0.256 = 0.6$

Event| Probability |Revenue (millions of dollars) 
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Win all $3$ games| $0.144$ |  $100$ 
Win the bowl game, but not all three games| $0.256$ | $20$ 
Other| $0.6$|  $10$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad\left(0.144 \cdot 100\right) + \left(0.256 \cdot 20\right)  + \left(0.6 \cdot 10\right) = 25.52$ million dollars
The expected revenue for The Wisconsin Badgers football team is $25.52$ million dollars.

assessment_items.question_content:
Denis is currently unemployed and wants to become the manager of Roberto's Restaurant. To become a manager in Roberto's Restaurant, Denis first has to serve as a cook there. To become a cook in Roberto's Restaurant, he first has to serve as a waiter there.

Denis has a $60\%$ chance of being hired as a waiter. If he becomes a waiter, he has a $50\%$ chance of being promoted to cook. If he becomes a cook, he has a $40\%$ chance of being promoted to manager. 

At Roberto's Restaurant, managers get paid $\$25$ per hour, cooks get paid $\$15$ per hour, and waiters get paid $\$10$ per hour. If Denis stays unemployed, he will make $\$0$ per hour.

**What is the expected hourly payoff of Denis's plan to seek employment at Roberto's Restaurant?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.   
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]] per hour
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible event.

The probability of Denis becoming a manager is:

$\qquad 0.6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.4  = 0.12$

The probability of Denis becoming a cook but not a manager is:

$\qquad 0.6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.6 = 0.18$

The probability of Denis becoming a waiter but not a cook is:

$\qquad 0.6 \cdot 0.5 = 0.3$ 

The probability of Denis staying unemployed is:

$\qquad 1 - 0.6 = 0.4$

Position| Probability | Hourly wage 
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Manager| $0.12$ |  $\$25$ 
Cook|   $0.18$ | $\$15$ 
Waiter| $0.3$|  $\$10$
Unemployed| $0.4$|  $\$0$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad\left(0.12 \cdot \$25\right) + \left(0.18 \cdot \$15\right)  + \left(0.3 \cdot \$10\right) + \left(0.4 \cdot \$0\right)$
The expected hourly payoff of Denis's plan to seek employment at Roberto's Restaurant is $\$8.70$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rory has entered The Irish Gunman’s Open. In The Irish Gunman’s Open, Rory will hit golf balls at targets. If he hits the target, he will advance to the next stage, and if he misses the target, Rory will exit The Open. The Open consists of $4$ possible stages.

If the best stage Rory completes is the second stage, he will win $100$ euros. If the best stage Rory completes is the third stage, Rory will win a total of $300$ euros. If he can complete all $4$ stages, he will win a total of $1000$ euros.

Because each stage gets progressively more difficult, the probability of Rory hitting the targets decreases. The probability of Rory hitting each target given that he has reached that stage is given in the data table below.

**What is the expected value of Rory's prize money from The Irish Gunman's Open?**  
Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.   
 [[☃ input-number 1]] euros


|||||
:- | :-: | :-: | :-: |:-: |
**Stage** | $1^\text{st}$ | $2^\text{nd}$| $3^\text{rd}$ | $4^\text{th}$
**Probability** | $60\%$ | $50\%$ | $40\%$ | $30\%$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible event.

The probability of Rory hitting all $4$ stages is:

$\qquad 0.6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.3  = 0.036$

The probability of Rory hitting the third stage but not the fourth stage is:

$\qquad 0.6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.7  = 0.084$

The probability of Rory hitting the second stage but not the third stage is:

$\qquad 0.6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.6 = 0.18$

The probability of any other event is:

$\qquad 1 - 0.18 - 0.084 - 0.036 = 0.7$

Best stage completed| Probability | Winnings
:-: | :-: | :-: 
$4^\text{th}$ | $0.036$ |  $1000$ euros
$3^\text{rd}$|  $0.084$ | $300$ euros 
$2^\text{nd}$| $0.18$|  $100$ euros
Other| $0.7$ | $0$ euros

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad\left(0.036 \cdot 1000 \right) + \left(0.084 \cdot 300\right)  + \left(0.18 \cdot 100\right) + \left(0.7 \cdot 0\right)  $
The expected value of Rory's winnings is $79.20$ euros.

assessment_items.question_content:
Laila is playing a game where there are $4$ blue markers and $6$ red markers in a box. She is going to pick $3$ markers, and replace each marker immediately after she picks it.

If she picks all $3$ red markers, she will win a total of $\$400$. If the first $2$ markers she picks are blue, she will win a total of $\$300$. Under any other outcome, she will win $\$0$.

**What is the expected value of Laila's winnings?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.   
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible event.

The probability of Laila picking all $3$ red markers is:

$\qquad$ **$\left(\dfrac{6}{10}\right)\left(\dfrac{6}{10}\right) \left(\dfrac{6}{10}\right) = 0.216$**

The probability of Laila picking $2$ blue markers is:

$\qquad$  **$\left(\dfrac{4}{10}\right)\left(\dfrac{4}{10}\right)  = 0.16$**

The probability of any other event is:

$\qquad 1 - 0.216 - 0.16 = 0.624$

Event| Probability | Winnings
:-: | :-: | :-: 
$3$ red markers | $0.216$ |  $\$400$ 
$2$ blue markers|   $0.16$ | $\$300$ 
Other| $0.624$|  $\$0$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad\left(0.216 \cdot \$400\right) + \left(0.16 \cdot \$300\right)  + \left(0.624 \cdot \$0\right) = \$134.40$
The expected value of Layla's winnings is $\$134.40$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nate is taking a math class, an English class, and a social studies class during his senior year of high school. Next year, he will be attending the University of North Carolina and studying to become an engineer. The $4$-year tuition for The University of North Carolina is $\$120{,}000$.

If Nate can earn an “A” in his math class, he will win a one-time $\$10{,}000$ math scholarship.  If he can earn an “A” in all three classes, he will win a $\$50{,}000$ one-time general scholarship in addition to the math scholarship.

His estimated probability of earning an “A” for each class is in the table below.

**What is the expected amount of tuition that Nate will pay to attend The University of North Carolina for $4$ years?**   
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]

||||
:- | :-: | :-: | :-: 
**Class** | Math | English | Social Studies
**Probability of an "A"** | $60\%$ | $30\%$ | $20\%$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible event.

The probability of Nate getting an "A" in all $3$ classes is:

$\qquad 0.6 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 0.2  = 0.036$

The probability of Nate getting an "A" in math class but not all $3$ classes is:

$\qquad 0.6 - 0.036  = 0.564$

The probability of Nate not getting any scholarships is:

$\qquad 1- 0.6= 0.4$

We also need to know how much Nate will pay under each event.

If Nate gets an "A" in all $3$ classes, he will receive the math scholarship and the general scholarship. Calculating the tuition he will pay gives:

 $\qquad\$120{,}000 - \$10{,}000 - \$50{,}000 = \$60{,}000$

If Nate gets an "A" in math class but not all $3$ classes, he will receive only the math scholarship. Calculating the tuition he will pay gives:

$\qquad\$120{,}000 - \$10{,}000 = \$110{,}000$

In all other situations, Nate will pay the full $4$-year tuition of $\$120{,}000$.

Putting the probabilities and tuitions together gives:

Scholarship| Probability | Tuition 
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Math and general | $0.036$ |  $\$60{,}000$ 
Only math|   $0.564$ | $\$110{,}000$  
Neither| $0.4$|  $\$120{,}000$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad\left(0.036 \cdot \$60{,}000\right) + \left(0.564 \cdot \$110{,}000\right)  + \left(0.4 \cdot \$120{,}000\right) $
The expected amount of tuition that Nate will pay is $ \$112{,}200$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Clumsy Carl is running a marathon.  There are $3$ dangerous areas on the course, and Clumsy Carl approximates that he has a $50\%$ chance of falling at each spot, independent of if he falls at the other spots.

The table below shows the total number of people that will see Carl fall based on how many times he falls.

**What is the expected total number of people that will see Carl fall?**  
Round your answer to the nearest hundred.  
[[☃ input-number 1]] people

|||||
:- | -: | -: | -: | -: 
**Falls**|$0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ 
**Total people** | $0$|$500$|$800$|$900$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible number of times that Carl falls.

Falls | Number of relevant outcomes |  Probability | Total people
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ |$1$ | $\dfrac{1}{\red{\text{8}}}$|$0$
$1$ |$3$| $\dfrac{3}{\red{\text{8}}}$ |$500$
$2$ |$3$ |$\dfrac{3}{\red{\text{8}}}$ |$800$
$3$ |$1$| $\dfrac{1}{\red{\text{8}}}$ |$900$
Total|$\red{\text{8}}$| $1.00$|

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{1}{\red{8}} \cdot 0\right) + \left(\dfrac{3}{\red{8}} \cdot 500\right) + \left(\dfrac{3}{\red{8}} \cdot 800\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{\red{8}} \cdot 900\right)$
The expected number of people to see Carl fall is $600$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Charlotte has just opened a bakery that is attempting to specialize in  French baguettes.  She has decided to sell baguettes in packages of $2$. 

Due to Charlotte's inexperience, she burns $40\%$ of the baguettes independent of each other. Instead of throwing out the burned baguettes, she offers them at a discount to her customers.

The table below shows the prices at which Charlotte sells a package of $2$ baguettes based on how many burned baguettes are in the package. (She packages baguettes as she produces them, without regard to sorting by type.) 

**What is the expected price of a package of $2$ baguettes?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]

||||
:- | -: | -: | -: 
**Burned baguettes**|$0$ | $1$ | $2$ | 
**Price of package** | $\$6$|$\$3$|$\$2$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible number of burned baguettes.

Letting "P" represent perfect baguettes and "B" represent burned baguettes, we can write out the sample space. 

Since the events aren't equally likely, we must use multiplication of probabilities for independent events to find the total probability for each number of burned baguettes.

Burned baguettes| Relevant outcomes | Probability
:-: | :-: | -: 
$0$ |{PP} | $0.6 \cdot 0.6 = 0.36$
$1$ |{PB, BP} | $0.6 \cdot 0.4 + 0.4 \cdot 0.6 = 0.48$
$2$ |{BB} | $0.4 \cdot 0.4 = 0.16$
Total | $4$ | $1.00$

Simplifying our table and including the price per package gives:

Burned baguettes| Probability | Price of package
:-: | :-: | :-: 
$0$ | $0.36$|$\$6$
$1$| $0.48$|$\$3$
$2$  | $0.16$|$\$2$
Total |$1.00$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad\left(0.36 \cdot \$6\right) + \left(0.48 \cdot \$3\right)  + \left(0.16 \cdot \$2\right)$
The expected price of a package of $2$ baguettes is $\$3.92$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lucas owns a car dealership in Brazil that is having a big sale for the next $3$ days. Lucas's car dealership will lose revenue if it rains because potential customers will stay at home. There is a $50\%$ chance of rain each of the next $3$ days independent of the other days.

The table below shows Lucas's potential revenue for his dealership's big sale depending on how many days it rains. 

**What is Lucas's car dealerships' expected revenue over the next $3$ days?**  
Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.  
 [[☃ input-number 1]] Brazilian real

|||||
:- | -: | -: | -: | -: 
**Rainy days**|$0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ 
**Revenue (Brazilian real)** | $3000$|$1800$|$1300$|$800$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible number of rainy days.

Rainy days | Number of relevant outcomes |  Probability | Revenue (Brazilian real)
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ |$1$ | $\dfrac{1}{\red{\text{8}}}$|$3000$
$1$ |$3$| $\dfrac{3}{\red{\text{8}}}$ |$1800$
$2$ |$3$ |$\dfrac{3}{\red{\text{8}}}$ |$1300$
$3$ |$1$| $\dfrac{1}{\red{\text{8}}}$ |$800$
Total|$\red{\text{8}}$| $1.00$|

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{1}{\red{8}} \cdot 3000\right) + \left(\dfrac{3}{\red{8}} \cdot 1800\right) + \left(\dfrac{3}{\red{8}} \cdot 1300\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{\red{8}} \cdot 800\right)$
Lucas's car dealerships' expected revenue over the next $3$ days is $1637.50$ Brazilian real.

assessment_items.question_content:
Irina has set up a lemonade stand for the next $2$ days.  When it rains, her lemonade stand has fewer customers. There is a $30\%$ chance of rain for each of the next $2$ days independent of the other days.  

The table below shows her earnings based on the number of rainy days.

**What is the expected earnings of Irina's lemonade stand over the next $2$ days?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]

||||
:- | -: | -: | -: 
**Rainy days**|$0$ | $1$ | $2$ | 
**Total earnings** | $\$100$|$\$60$|$\$20$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible number of rainy days.

Letting "C" represent a clear day and "R" represent a rainy day, we can write out the sample space.

 Since the events aren't equally likely, we must use multiplication of probabilities for independent events to find the total probability for each number of rainy days.

Rainy days| Relevant outcomes | Probability
:-: | :-: | -: 
$0$ |{CC} | $0.7 \cdot 0.7 = 0.49$
$1$ |{CR, RC} | $0.7 \cdot 0.3 + 0.3 \cdot 0.7 = 0.42$
$2$ |{RR} | $0.3 \cdot 0.3 = 0.09$
Total | $4$ | $1.00$

Simplifying our table and including the total earnings gives:

Rainy days| Probability | Total earnings
:-: | :-: | :-: 
$0$ | $0.49$|$\$100$
$1$| $0.42$|$\$60$
$2$  | $0.09$|$\$20$
Total |$1.00$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad\left(0.49 \cdot \$100\right) + \left(0.42 \cdot \$60\right)  + \left(0.09 \cdot \$20\right)$
The expected earnings for Irina's lemonade stand are $\$76$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Adam is entering The Hammer County Firework Contest with $4$ high intensity fireworks.  Each firework has a $50\%$ chance of being a dud and therefore not leaving the ground independent of the other fireworks.

The table below shows how much Adam will win depending on how many of his fireworks are duds.


**What is  Adam's expected winnings from The Hammer County Firework Contest?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]

||||||
:- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -: | 
**Duds**|$0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |$4$
**Winnings** | $\$1000$|$\$700$|$\$500$|$\$100$|$\$0$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible number of duds.

Since each outcome has equal probability, we can use the following formula:

$\qquad  \text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Relevant outcomes}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

Since Adam has $4$ different fireworks that can either work or be a dud, the size of the sample space is $2^4 = \red{16}$.

To find relevant outcomes for small sample spaces, we can write out the sample space and count. 

Letting "W" represent fireworks that work and "D" represent fireworks that are duds, the sample space for Adam lighting off $4$ fireworks is:

Duds| Relevant outcomes
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | {WWWW}| 
 $1$|{WWWD, WWDW, WDWW, DWWW}|
$2$|{DDWW, DWDW, DWWD, WDDW, WDWD, WWDD}  | 
  $3$|{DDDW, DDWD, DWDD, WDDD}  | 
$4$|{DDDD} 
Total |$\red{16}$
$\qquad$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{1}{\red{16}} \cdot \$1000\right) + \left(\dfrac{4}{\red{16}} \cdot \$700\right) + \left(\dfrac{6}{\red{16}} \cdot \$500\right) + \left(\dfrac{4}{\red{16}} \cdot \$100\right)+ \left(\dfrac{1}{\red{16}} \cdot \$0\right)$
The expected value of Adam's  winnings from The Hammer County Firework Contest is $\$450$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Laila is visiting the grounds of an ancient burial site in the shape of a pyramid and is hoping to find old buried artifacts and sell them to a museum.

Historians have told her that there is only $1$ undiscovered artifact per side of the pyramid. She plans to look on all $4$ sides of the pyramid and assumes that on each side there is a $50\%$ chance of her finding that side’s artifact independent of the other sides.  

The table below shows the resale value depending on how many artifacts she finds.

**What is the expected value of the resale value of the artifacts Laila finds?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]


||||||
:- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -: | 
**Artifacts found**|$0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |$4$
**Resale value** | $\$0$|$\$100$|$\$250$|$\$500$|$\$1000$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible number of artifacts that Laila finds.

Since each outcome has equal probability, we can use the following formula:

$\qquad  \text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Relevant outcomes}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

Since there are $4$ different sides to the pyramid where Laila can either find an artifact or not, the size of the sample space is $2^4 = \red{16}$.

To find relevant outcomes for small sample spaces, we can write out the sample space and count. 

Letting "N" represent not finding an artifact and "A" represent finding an artifact, the sample space for Laila searching for artifacts on the $4$ sides of a pyramid is:

Artifacts found| Relevant outcomes
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | {NNNN}| 
 $1$|{NNNA, NNAN, NANN, ANNN}|
$2$|{AANN, ANAN, ANNA, NAAN, NANA, NNAA}  | 
  $3$|{AAAN, AANA, ANAA, NAAA}  | 
$4$|{AAAA} 
Total |$\red{16}$
$\qquad$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{1}{\red{16}} \cdot \$0\right) + \left(\dfrac{4}{\red{16}} \cdot \$100\right) + \left(\dfrac{6}{\red{16}} \cdot \$250\right) + \left(\dfrac{4}{\red{16}} \cdot \$500\right)+ \left(\dfrac{1}{\red{16}} \cdot \$1000\right)$
The expected resale value of Laila's artifacts is $\$306.25$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Frank's in-laws have told him they might be visiting for Thanksgiving and Christmas. To prepare, Frank bought his groceries in bulk this month. If Frank's in-laws don't come for both Thanksgiving and Christmas, some of his groceries will spoil.

For each holiday, Frank estimates that there is a $70\%$ chance that his in-laws will visit independent of the other holiday.  The table below shows the value of spoiled groceries based on how many holidays Frank's in-laws will visit him.


**What is the expected cost of spoiled groceries?**  
Consider cost to be positive and round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]

||||
:- | -: | -: | -: 
**In-law holiday visits**|$0$ | $1$ | $2$ | 
**Cost of spoiled groceries** | $\$50$|$\$20$|$\$0$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible number of in-law holiday visits.

Letting "S" represent Frank's in-laws staying at home and "V" represent Frank's in-laws visiting, we can write out the sample space. 

Since the events aren't equally likely, we must use multiplication of probabilities for independent events to find the total probability for each number of holiday visits.

In-law holiday visits| Relevant outcomes | Probability
:-: | :-: | -: 
$0$ |{SS} | $0.3 \cdot 0.3 = 0.09$
$1$ |{SV, VS} | $0.3 \cdot 0.7 + 0.3 \cdot 0.7 = 0.42$
$2$ |{VV} | $0.7 \cdot 0.7 = 0.49$
Total | $4$ | $1.00$

Simplifying our table and including the cost of spoiled groceries gives:

In-law holiday visits| Probability | Cost of spoiled groceries
:-: | :-: | :-: 
$0$ | $0.09$|$\$50$
$1$| $0.42$|$\$20$
$2$  | $0.49$|$\$0$
Total |$1.00$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad\left(0.09 \cdot \$50\right) + \left(0.42 \cdot \$20\right)  + \left(0.49 \cdot \$0\right)$
The expected cost of Frank's spoiled groceries is $\$12.90$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Marvin the monkey is taking a multiple choice test as part of an experiment. There are $4$ questions on the test and each question has $2$ different answer choices. Since Marvin is a monkey, he will be guessing on each question. 

The test is graded according to the grading scheme below.

**What is the expected number of points Marvin will score?**  
Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.  
 [[☃ input-number 1]] points

||||||
:- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -: | 
**Correct answers**|$0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ 
**Points** | $0$|$3$|$5$|$7$|$10$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible number of questions that Marvin answers correct.

Since each outcome has equal probability, we can use the following formula:

$\qquad  \text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Relevant outcomes}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

Since there are $4$ questions and $2$ answers that Marvin will be guessing between for each question, the size of the sample space is $2^4 = \red{16}$.

To find relevant outcomes for small sample spaces, we can write out the sample space and count. 

Letting "I"  represent questions that Marvin answers incorrectly and "C" represent questions that Marvin answers correctly, the sample space for Marvin guessing on $4$ questions is:



Correct answers| Relevant outcomes
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | {IIII}| 
 $1$|{IIIC, IICI, ICII, CIII}|
$2$|{CCII, CICI, CIIC, ICCI, ICIC, IICC}  | 
  $3$|{CCCI, CCIC, CICC, ICCC}  | 
$4$|{CCCC} 
Total |$\red{16}$
$\qquad$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{1}{\red{16}} \cdot 0\right) + \left(\dfrac{4}{\red{16}} \cdot 3\right) + \left(\dfrac{6}{\red{16}} \cdot 5\right) + \left(\dfrac{4}{\red{16}} \cdot 7\right)+ \left(\dfrac{1}{\red{16}} \cdot 10\right)$
Marvin the monkey is expected to score $5$ points on the test.

assessment_items.question_content:
Martha has planted $4$ root vegetables to make chili to enter into The Southwest Chili Cook-off. 

Each vegetable that she plants has a $50\%$ chance of successfully growing independent of the other vegetables. For each unsuccessful vegetable, she will have to use canned vegetables, which will be much less tasty, and result in less prize winnings for Martha.

The table below shows how much Martha will win in the cook-off based on the number of successfully grown vegetables.

**What is Martha's expected winnings from The Southwest Chili Cook-off?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]

||||||
:- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -: | 
**Successful vegetables**|$0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |$4$
**Winnings** | $\$0$|$\$10$|$\$50$|$\$75$|$\$150$
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability and value for each possible number of successful vegetables.

Since each outcome has equal probability, we can use the following formula:

$\qquad  \text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Relevant outcomes}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

Since Martha is planting $4$ different vegatables that can either be unsuccessful or successful, the size of the sample space is $2^4 = \red{16}$.

To find relevant outcomes for small sample spaces, we can write out the sample space and count.


Letting "U"  represent unsuccessful vegetables and "S" represent successful vegetables, the sample space for Martha planting $4$ vegetables is:

Successful vegetables| Relevant outcomes
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | {UUUU}| 
 $1$|{UUUS, UUSU, USUU, SUUU}|
$2$|{SSUU, SUSU, SUUS, USSU, USUS, UUSS}  | 
  $3$|{SSSU, SSUS, SUSS, USSS}  | 
$4$|{SSSS} 
Total |$\red{16}$
$\qquad$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{1}{\red{16}} \cdot \$0\right) + \left(\dfrac{4}{\red{16}} \cdot \$10\right) + \left(\dfrac{6}{\red{16}} \cdot \$50\right) + \left(\dfrac{4}{\red{16}} \cdot \$75\right)+ \left(\dfrac{1}{\red{16}} \cdot \$150\right)$
Martha's expected winnings from the cook-off are $\$49.38$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Vera has challenged Alexey to a round of Marker Mixup.  Marker Mixup is a game where there is a bag of $5$ red markers numbered $1$ through $5$, and another bag with $5$ green markers numbered $6$ through $10$.  

Alexey will grab $1$ marker from each bag, and if the $2$ markers add up to more than $12$, he will win $\$5$. If the sum is exactly $12$, he will break even, and If the sum is less than $12$, he will lose $\$6$.

**What is Alexey's expected value of playing Marker Mixup?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability of each possible value.

There are a total of $5 \cdot 5 = \red{25}$ ways to grab $2$ markers.

Alexey wins when the sum is greater than $12$. The table below shows all of the possible outcomes. By counting numbers greater than $12$, we find that there are $6$ different ways for Alexey to win.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Alexey wins}) = \dfrac{6}{\red{25}}$


 | *$\green{6}$* | *$\green{7}$* | *$\green{8}$* | *$\green{9}$*| *$\green{10}$*
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | 
*$\red{1}$* | $7$ | $8$|$9$|$10$|$11$
*$\red{2}$* | $8$ | $9$|$10$|$11$|$12$
*$\red{3}$* | $9$|$10$|$11$|$12$|$13$
*$\red{4}$*| $10$|$11$|$12$|$13$|$14$
*$\red{5}$*| $11$| $12$ | $13$|$14$|$15$

Alexey ties when the sum is equal to $12$.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Alexey ties}) = \dfrac{4}{\red{25}}$

Alexey must lose the other $\red{25} - 6 - 4 = 15$ ways.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Alexey loses}) = \dfrac{15}{\red{25}}$

Summarizing what we have so far:

Event| Probability|Value
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Alexey wins |$\dfrac{6}{\red{25}}$ | $\$5$
Alexey ties | $\dfrac{4}{\red{25}}$ | $\$0$
Alexey loses | $\dfrac{15}{\red{25}}$ | $-\$6$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{6}{\red{25}} \cdot \$5\right) + \left(\dfrac{4}{\red{25}} \cdot \$0\right)  + \left(\dfrac{15}{\red{25}} \cdot -\$6\right)$
The expected value of Alexey playing Marker Mixup is $-\$2.40$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rani and Kajal are playing a card game using a standard $52$ card deck. 

Kajal will pick a card at random. If the card is a black card, she will win $\$5$. If the card is a face card (jack, queen, or king), but not a black card, she will win $\$3$. If she picks anything else, she will lose $\$10$.

The image below shows a standard deck of playing cards.

**What is Kajal's expected value of playing this game?**  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]


[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

We need to find the probability of each possible value.

There are a total of $\red{52}$ cards in a deck.

Kajal wins the most when he draws a black card. Counting from the image given in the question, there are $26$ black cards in a deck. 

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Black card}) = \dfrac{26}{\red{52}}$

Kajal also wins when he chooses a face card that is not black. There are $6$ face cards that are not black cards in a deck.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Face card and not black}) = \dfrac{6}{\red{52}}$

Kajal loses when he draws one of the other $\red{52} - 26 - 6 = 20$ cards.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Other}) = \dfrac{20}{\red{52}}$

Summarizing what we have so far:

Event| Probability|Value
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Black card |$\dfrac{26}{\red{52}}$ | $\$5$
Face card and not black | $\dfrac{6}{\red{52}}$ | $\$3$
Other | $\dfrac{20}{\red{52}}$ | $-\$10$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{26}{\red{52}} \cdot \$5\right) + \left(\dfrac{6}{\red{52}} \cdot \$3\right)  + \left(\dfrac{20}{\red{52}} \cdot -\$10\right)$
The expected value of Kajal playing this card game is $-\$1$.

options.alt: A full deck of cards shown as 12 columns and 4 rows. Each row is a different suit. Each column is a different number or face of a card.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sam the squirrel monkey is a homebuilder. He is randomly choosing paint and siding colors for a house he is working on.  The homeowner wants the paint and siding colors to match. If they do not match, Sam will have to redo the home which will be costly.

Sam has $6$ paint options: red, green, white, black, purple, and yellow. He has $3$ siding options: red, green, and white.

 If Sam chooses the same color for both the paint and siding, the homeowner will be happy, and Sam will earn $\$1000$.  If Sam chooses colors that aren’t the same, he will lose $\$100$.  


**What is Sam’s expected value of painting and siding this house?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability of each possible value.

Since there are $6$ colors of paint and $3$ colors of siding, there are $6 \cdot 3 = \red{18}$ combinations of paint and siding colors.

The homeowner will be happy when both the paint and siding matches. By counting matches from the table below, we find that there are $3$ different ways for this to happen.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Homeowner happy}) = \dfrac{3}{\red{18}}$

 | Red | Green | White | Black| Purple | Yellow 
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |  :-:
**Red** | **RR** | RG|RW| RB| RP | RY
**Green** | GR | **GG**|GW | GB| GP| GY
**White** | WR|WG|**WW**|WB| WP|WY
*

Under any other outcome, the homeowner is unhappy. There are $\red{18} - 3 = 15$ other combinations of paint and siding colors.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Homeowner unhappy}) = \dfrac{15}{\red{18}}$

Summarizing what we have so far:

Event| Probability|Value
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Homeowner happy |$\dfrac{3}{\red{18}}$ | $\$1000$
Homeowner unhappy | $\dfrac{15}{\red{18}}$ | $-\$100$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{3}{\red{18}} \cdot \$1000\right) + \left(\dfrac{15}{\red{18}} \cdot -\$100\right)$
The expected value of Sam painting and siding this home is $\$83.33$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Merita has decided to play in The Clothing Combo Contest.  First, she will randomly choose from a pair of brown, purple, blue, green, or black pants. Next, she will randomly choose from a black, brown, or green shirt.  

If both the shirt and pants are a color that starts with a "B", she will win $\$10$.  If only one of the pieces of clothing is a color that starts with a "B", she will break even.  Under any other outcome, she will lose $\$20$.

**What is Merita’s expected value of playing The Clothing Combo Contest?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability of each possible value.

Since there are $5$ colors of pants and $3$ colors of shirts, there are $5 \cdot 3 = \red{15}$ outfits that Merita can choose. 

Merita wins when both the shirt and pants that she chooses starts with a "B". By counting "BB" from the table below, we find that there are $6$ different ways for this to happen.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Merita wins}) = \dfrac{6}{\red{15}}$

 | Black | Brown | Green 
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | 
**Brown** | **BB** | **BB**|BG|
**Purple** | PB | PB|PG
**Blue** | **BB**|**BB**|BG
**Green**| GB|GB|GG
**Black**| **BB**| **BB** | BG|

Merita ties when the outfit only has $1$ "B".

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Merita ties}) = \dfrac{7}{\red{15}}$

Merita must lose the other $\red{15} - 6 - 7 = 2$ times.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Merita loses}) = \dfrac{2}{\red{15}}$

Summarizing what we have so far:

Event| Probability|Value
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Merita wins |$\dfrac{6}{\red{15}}$ | $\$10$
Merita ties | $\dfrac{7}{\red{15}}$ | $\$0$
Merita loses | $\dfrac{2}{\red{15}}$ | $-\$20$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{6}{\red{15}} \cdot \$10\right) + \left(\dfrac{7}{\red{15}} \cdot \$0\right)  + \left(\dfrac{2}{\red{15}} \cdot -\$20\right)$
The expected value of Merita playing The Clothing Combo Contest is $1.33$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Vlad is playing a dice game with Ulga. 

Vlad will roll $2$ six-sided dice, and if the sum of the dice is divisible by $3$, he will win $\$6$.  If the sum is not divisible by $3$, he will lose $\$3$.

**What is Vlad’s expected value of playing this game?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability of each possible value.

There are a total of $6 \cdot 6 = \red{36}$ ways to roll $2$ dice.

Vlad wins when the sum is divisible by $3$. By counting from the table below, we find that there are $12$ different ways for this to happen.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Vlad wins}) = \dfrac{12}{\red{36}}$


[[☃ image 1]]

Vlad must lose the other $\red{36} - 12 = 24$ ways.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Vlad loses}) = \dfrac{24}{\red{36}}$

Summarizing what we have so far:

Event| Probability|Value
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Vlad wins |$\dfrac{12}{\red{36}}$ | $\$6$
Vlad loses | $\dfrac{24}{\red{36}}$ | $-\$3$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{12}{\red{36}} \cdot \$6\right)  + \left(\dfrac{24}{\red{36}} \cdot -\$3\right)$
The expected value of Vlad playing this dice game is $\$0$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bob and Anna are planning to meet for lunch at Sally's Restaurant, but they forgot to schedule a time. Bob and Anna are each going to randomly choose from either $1\text{ p.m.}$, $2\text{ p.m.}$, $3\text{ p.m.}$, or $4\text{ p.m.}$ to show up at Sally's Restaurant. They must both choose exactly the same time in order to meet.

Bob has a “buy one entree, get one entree free” coupon that he can only use if he meets up with Anna.

If he successfully meets with Anna, Bob's lunch will cost him $\$5$. If they do not meet, Bob's lunch will cost him $\$10$.  

**What is the expected cost of Bob's lunch?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability of each possible value.

There are a total of $4 \cdot 4 = \red{16}$ time combinations.

Bob will get to use his coupon if they meet. By looking at the table below, we see that there are $4$ different ways for them to meet.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Bob and Anna meet}) = \dfrac{4}{\red{16}}$

 | $1\text{ p.m.}$ | $2\text{ p.m.}$ | $3\text{ p.m.}$ | $4\text{ p.m.}$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | 
$1\text{ p.m.}$ | $1,1$ | $1,2$|$1,3$|$1,4$
$2\text{ p.m.}$ | $2,1$ | $2,2$|$2,3$|$2,4$
$3\text{ p.m.}$ | $3,1$|$3,2$|$3,3$|$3,4$
$4\text{ p.m.}$ | $4,1$|$4,2$|$4,3$|$4,4$

They must not meet the other $\red{16} - 4 = 12$ time combinations.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Bob and Anna don't meet}) = \dfrac{12}{\red{16}}$

Summarizing what we have so far:

Event| Probability|Cost of Bob's lunch
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Bob and Anna meet |$\dfrac{4}{\red{16}}$ | $\$5$
Bob and Anna don't meet | $\dfrac{12}{\red{16}}$| $\$10$

We can now write our expected cost expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{4}{\red{16}} \cdot \$5\right) + \left(\dfrac{12}{\red{16}} \cdot \$10\right)$
The expected cost of Bob's lunch is $\$8.75$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Pooja and Amit are playing a card game with a standard $52$ card deck. 

Pooja will draw a card. If the card she draws is a face card (a jack, queen, or king), she will win $\$6$. If Pooja draws an ace, she will win $\$4$.  If any other card is drawn, she will lose $\$2$.

The image below shows a standard deck of playing cards.

**What is Pooja's expected value of playing this game?**  
Round your answer to the nearest cent.  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1b1398eb650f7f695038f767fdaa97ee.jpeg)
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability of each possible value.

There are a total of $\red{52}$ cards in a deck.

Pooja wins the most when she draws a face card. Counting from the image given in the question, there are $12$ face cards in a deck.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Face card}) = \dfrac{12}{\red{52}}$

Pooja also wins when she draws an ace. There are $4$ aces in a deck.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Ace}) = \dfrac{4}{\red{52}}$

Pooja loses when she draws one of the other $\red{52} - 12 - 4 = 36$ cards.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Other}) = \dfrac{36}{\red{52}}$

Summarizing what we have so far:

Event| Probability|Value
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Face card |$\dfrac{12}{\red{52}}$ | $\$6$
Ace | $\dfrac{4}{\red{52}}$ | $\$4$
Other | $\dfrac{36}{\red{52}}$ | $-\$2$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{12}{\red{52}} \cdot \$6\right) + \left(\dfrac{4}{\red{52}} \cdot \$4\right)  + \left(\dfrac{36}{\red{52}} \cdot -\$2\right)$
The expected value of Pooja playing this card game is $\$0.31$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Cecilia has created a new game for her friend, Lyla.  Lyla will grab a ball from a bucket with $3$ red balls numbered $1$ through $3$, and another ball from a bucket with $7$ green balls numbered $1$ through $7$.  

If Lyla pulls an odd numbered ball from both of the buckets, she will win $\$10$.  If she pulls an odd numbered red ball from the first bucket, but an even green ball from the second bucket, Lyla will break even. Under any other outcome, Lyla will lose $\$8$.

**What is Lyla’s expected value of playing this game? Round your answer to the nearest cent.**  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability of each possible value.

There are a total of $3 \cdot 7 = \blue{21}$ ways to grab $2$ balls.

Lyla wins when she pulls an odd numbered ball from first the red bucket then the green bucket. The table below shows all of the possible outcomes, where "$O$" represents an odd numbered ball and "E" represents an even numbered ball.

By counting ways to draw an odd numbered ball on both attempts or ${O,O}$, we find that there are $8$ different ways for Lyla to win.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Lyla wins}) = \dfrac{8}{\blue{21}}$


$$ | *${1}$* | *${2}$* | *${3}$* | *${4}$*| *${5}$* | *${6}$*| *${7}$*
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | 
*${1}$* | ${O},{O}$ | $O,E$|${O},{O}$|${O},{E}$|${O},{O}$| ${O},{E}$ | ${O},{O}$ |
*${2}$* | ${E},{O}$ | ${E},{E}$|${E},{O}$|${E},{E}$|${E},{O}$ | ${E},{E}$ | ${E},{O}$
*${3}$* | ${O},{O}$|${O},{E}$|${O},{O}$|${O},{E}$|${O},{O}$ | ${O},{E}$| ${O},{O}$

Lyla ties when she draws an odd numbered red ball and an even numbered green ball or ${O},{E}$.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Lyla ties}) = \dfrac{6}{\blue{21}}$

Lyla must lose the other $\blue{21} - 8 - 6 = 7$ ways.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Lyla loses}) = \dfrac{7}{\blue{21}}$

Summarizing what we have so far:

Event| Probability|Value
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Lyla wins |$\dfrac{8}{\blue{21}}$ | $\$10$
Lyla ties | $\dfrac{6}{\blue{21}}$ | $\$0$
Lyla loses | $\dfrac{7}{\blue{21}}$ | $-\$8$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{8}{\blue{21}} \cdot \$10\right) + \left(\dfrac{6}{\blue{21}} \cdot \$0\right)  + \left(\dfrac{7}{\blue{21}} \cdot -\$8\right)$
The expected value of Lyla playing this game is $\$1.14$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jorge is playing a game at his local casino where he flips a coin and then rolls a $6$-sided die.

 If the coin is heads and the die is a number greater than $2$, he will win $\$8$.  If the coin is heads and the die is exactly $2$, he will break
even. Under any other outcome, he will lose $\$2$.

**What is Jorge's expected value of playing this game? Round your answer to the nearest cent.**  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.


We need to find the probability of each possible value.

There are a total of $2 \cdot 6 = \red{12}$ ways to flip a coin and roll a die.

Jorge wins when he flips heads and the die is greater than $2$. By counting from the table below, we find that there are $4$ different ways for this to happen.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Jorge wins}) = \dfrac{4}{\red{12}}$

|$1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$| $5$| $6$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | 
**Heads** | H$1$ | H$2$|H$3$|H$4$|H$5$ | H$6$
**Tails** | T$1$ | T$2$|T$3$|T$4$|T$5$| T$6$

Jorge ties when the coin is heads and the die is exactly $2$. This happens only once.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Jorge ties}) = \dfrac{1}{\red{12}}$

Jorge must lose the other $\red{12} - 4 - 1 = 7$ ways.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Jorge loses}) = \dfrac{7}{\red{12}}$

Summarizing what we have so far:

Event| Probability|Value
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Jorge wins |$\dfrac{4}{\red{12}}$ | $\$8$
Jorge ties | $\dfrac{1}{\red{12}}$ | $\$0$
Jorge loses | $\dfrac{7}{\red{12}}$ | $-\$2$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{4}{\red{12}} \cdot \$8\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{\red{12}} \cdot \$0\right)  + \left(\dfrac{7}{\red{12}} \cdot -\$2\right)$
The expected value of Jorge playing this coin and die game is $\$1.50$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Carlos is playing a dice game with Elena.

 Carlos will roll $2$ six-sided dice, and if the sum is greater than $7$, he will win $\$5$. If the sum is $7$, they will tie and he will break even. If the sum is less than $7$, he will lose $\$4$. 

**What is Carlos's expected value of playing this game? Round your answer to the nearest cent.**  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding $\text{probability} \cdot \text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

We need to find the probability of each possible value.

There are a total of $6 \cdot 6 = \red{36}$ ways to roll $2$ dice.

Carlos wins when the sum is greater than $7$. By counting from the table below, we find that there are $15$ different ways for this to happen.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Carlos wins}) = \dfrac{15}{\red{36}}$


[[☃ image 1]]

Carlos ties when the sum is equal to $7$.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Carlos ties}) = \dfrac{6}{\red{36}}$

Carlos must lose the other $\red{36} - 15 - 6 = 15$ times.

$\qquad$  $P(\text{Carlos loses}) = \dfrac{15}{\red{36}}$

Summarizing what we have so far:

Event| Probability|Value
:-: | :-: | :-: 
Carlos wins |$\dfrac{15}{\red{36}}$ | $\$5$
Carlos ties | $\dfrac{6}{\red{36}}$ | $\$0$
Carlos loses | $\dfrac{15}{\red{36}}$ | $-\$4$

We can now write our expected value expression:

$\qquad$ $\left(\dfrac{15}{\red{36}} \cdot \$5\right) + \left(\dfrac{6}{\red{36}} \cdot \$0\right)  + \left(\dfrac{15}{\red{36}} \cdot -\$4\right)$
The expected value of Carlos playing this dice game is $\$0.42$.
